architecture.summary: The paper proves theoretically that Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an RL algorithm for LLM fine-tuning that uses outcome-level rewards and relative advantage computation within trajectory groups, is mathematically equivalent to a Process Reward Model (PRM)-aware objective. The key mechanism is that when trajectories within a group share overlapping prefixes, GRPO implicitly assigns step-level (process) rewards computed as Monte Carlo estimates—the mean outcome reward of all trajectories sharing each prefix. The paper then proposes λ-GRPO, which adds a process-step-frequency normalization factor to mitigate a discovered flaw in GRPO's advantage computation that can hinder exploration and exploitation under imbalanced process step frequencies.
trained_components: LLM policy (during fine-tuning with GRPO or λ-GRPO)

biblio.title: GRPO is Secretly a Process Reward Model

claims.statement: GRPO equipped with an outcome reward model is mathematically equivalent to a PRM-aware RL objective equipped with a non-trivial Monte-Carlo-based PRM, where process rewards are computed as the mean outcome reward of trajectories sharing each prefix, given mild assumptions (DAPO token-level objective and β fixed per batch).
GRPO's implicit PRM is non-trivial in practice: overlapping trajectory prefixes within groups arise very frequently under real-world conditions.

In real-world GRPO training on OpenRS math-reasoning dataset with group sizes of 6 and 36, 99.8% of groups generated non-trivial process reward structures (6,688 out of 6,700 groups) with group size 6, and 100% (1,100 out of 1,100 groups) with group size 36.
GRPO's advantage computation has a flaw that interacts with imbalanced process step frequencies and rewards to hinder both exploration and exploitation under different conditions.

On a synthetic binary-tree environment with imbalanced process rewards, λ-GRPO converges on the target high-reward path more frequently than standard GRPO across all hyperparameter configurations tested (ε ∈ {-0.5, -1.0, -1.5}, K ∈ {1, 2}).

λ-GRPO-tuned models outperformed standard GRPO on 15 out of 20 downstream reasoning task cells (AIME24, MATH-500, AMC23, Minerva, OlympiadBench) across two model sizes (Qwen-1.5B and Llama-1B) and two KL coefficient settings.

claims.metric: validation accuracy (exact-match on OpenRS); training steps to peak
claims.statement: All four λ-GRPO models reached higher validation accuracy in fewer training steps than their GRPO-tuned counterparts, with λ-GRPO representing more than 10% increase in validation accuracy in less than half the number of training steps on average.
λ-GRPO introduces negligible computational overhead: measured wall-clock overhead on a single Intel i7 CPU was 8.38–10.27 seconds per training run (amounting to ~1e-6–1e-5 seconds/token).
